title: practical english usage - Part 315
content:
But ever is sometimes used to mean ‘ always’  in compound expressions with not I shall always remember you. (     I shall ever remember you.) Ever is not normally used to mean ‘ always’ .

5 ever     meaning       'always' Have you ever been to Africa? (The hearer is not in Africa.) Ever (without before) does not refer to a present event.

Have you (ever) been to Scotland before? (The hearer is probably in Scotland.) it has happened at another time.

is a difference. Before (or ever before) refers to a present event, and asks whether Ever and before can both be used to mean ‘ at any time in the past’ , but there 4 ever     and   before;       ever     before Word  Problems from A  to Z      Section 31 vocabulary’  •  457  expect, hope, wait and lookforward somewhere until something happens.

Waiting is often physical - the word suggests, for example, standing or sitting something, or when one wants time to pass so that something will happen.

One waits when somebody or something is late, when one is early for 2  expect       and   wait:    difference        of  meaning I expect it will rain at the weekend. But I hope it won't.

One can expect good or bad things, but one only hopes for things that one wants.

She's hoping it will be a girl.

- Lucy's expecting a baby. (= She's pregnant.) I hope he's got some good news.

- I'm expecting Jack to phone at three o'clock.

not know whether it will. Compare: emotional. If 1 hope for something to happen, I would like it to happen, but I do happen, I have a good reason to think it will in fact happen. Hoping is more Expecting is a matter of thinking rather than feeling. If I expect something to 1  expect        and   hope:     difference        of  meaning 457 expect,       hope,      wait     and   look     forward For the use of but to mean 'except', ►    4)3.

I ttHHild-httveftiiledz) not Without / But for your help, I would have failed, (     Except for your help, Nobody helped me except you.

In other cases, without or but for may be better. Compare: Except (for) is only used to talk about exceptions to generalisations.

6 except        and   without You needn't worry about anything except having a great time.

She's not interested in anything except skiing.

In other cases an -ing form is usually necessary.

I'll do everything for you except cook.

He does nothing except eat all day.

+ infinitive               without A  common structure is do ... except         to.

5 except        + verb:   He  does      nothing         except       eat.

We're all ready except (for) her.

Everybody understood except (for) me. (     .. . except I.) After except (for) we  use object pronouns, not subject pronouns.

4 except        (for)   + pronoun The trip was nice except that there wasn't enough snow.

This room is no use except as a storeroom.

He's good-looking except when he smiles.

It's the same everywhere except in Scotland, (     ...excepFfor-m-Scetinnd.) We   use except, not except for, before prepositions and conjunctions.

3 except        before     prepositions         and   conjunctions vocabulary' •  457  expect, hope, wait and look forward leave home.                               -> I look forward / I'm looking forward to the day when the children We expect / We're expecting to hear from Lucy today.

We hope / We're hoping to get to Scotland next weekend.

forward to can often be used with little difference of meaning.

Before an infinitive, simple and progressive forms of hope, expect and look 6 simple     and   progressive         forms I look forward to hearing from you. (in an email / a letter) not I look forward to talking to him. (     ... to talk to him.) After look forward to, we  use the -ing form of a verb (►   104.2).

I'm still waiting for him  to pay me back that money.

I'm hoping for him  to come up with some new ideas.

I expect Jack to arrive about ten o'clock, (     I expect for him  to arrive...) I'm looking forward to the autumn.

We've been waiting for rain for weeks.

We're hoping for a lot of rain - the garden's very dry.

We're expecting rain soon.
